    Feminist Data Principles


    Data and bodies



















                                                                                            2

If      critical data  studies investigates
power, critical data  visualization
continues this with two key questions

W   hat is the data being visualised?

How can we cr eate mor e r esponsible
data visualizations?









                                                                   3

                     Data V isualization
                     Liter  acy



                                                           Pinney  (2020) argues that data visualization
                                                           can offer a way   to develop data literacy –
                                                           and in turn offer a way  solution to uneven
                                                           data literacies
                                                           Literacy  can be an enabler of social change
                                                           – raising questions about power,  and
                                                           enabling those with  less power  to ask critical
                                                           questions












Critical Data Visualization                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         4

    Feminist Data
    V isualization

      1.                                         Rethink binaries – representation strategies based on multiplicity rather
            than binaries
      2.                                         Embrace pluralism –                                                                                                         challenges notions of objectivity, neutrality, and
            universalism
      3.                                         Examiner power  and aspire to empower – rejecting hierarchal power
            structures (e.g. patriarchy)  and systems of subject/object -
            acknowledges   the user as a source of knowledge
      4.                                        Consider context – all knowledge   is situated (social, cultural, and
            material context matters!)
      5.                                         Legitimize embodiment and affect – create emotional bonds with  data
            stories and acknowledge   the body









D’Ignazio, C., & Klein, L. F. (2016).         Feminist  Data Visualization.                                                                                                                      5

                   Rethink Binaries

Binary  does not just refer
to gender
Nature vs culture
Subject vs object
Reason vs emotion


This example  illustrates
issues with visualizing
binaries


It introduces ‘people’  as
the voting object as
opposed  to ‘states’

                        Design and Binaries





































Source: https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/2020/10/30/opinion/election-                                                                                                      results - maps.html                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   7

Design Process Questions: Is our data the right type?  What categories have we  taken for
granted? How  can we  register responses that do not fit into the categories we  have
provided, even and especially  if they are “edge cases” and “outliers”?


Design Output Questions: How  do we  communicate the limits of our categories in the final
representation? How  can we  allow  the user to refactor the categories we  have presented
for view?                                                                                                                                  8

Embr  ace
Plur  alism



Considers subjectivity – who
is creating the visualization?
Who are they making it for?


More than just ‘producer  ’ and
‘audience’  it considers
‘subjectivity’  over ‘objectivity’













                                                                                                                           9

   Embr  ace Plur  alism






  Design Process Questions: Whose voices are not represented on
  the design team but might be important for the conceptualization  of
  the project? Who is being envisioned  as the ideal user? How  could
  additional perspectives be accommodated, even those considered
  marginal? Whose perspectives have been excluded  from the
  categorization schema? For example, collecting gender in
  female/male buckets excludes transgender, gender-fluid and two-
  spirit people.


  Design Output Questions: Can the artifact communicate the subject
  positions of the researcher(s) and designer(s) in a transparent way?
  Whose view  of the world  does the visualization  represent?




D’Ignazio, C., & Klein, L. F. (2016).        Feminist Data                                                                           10
Visualization      .

     Examine Power
     Aspir e  to Empower


Queering the Map is a
method of recording –
and sharing –
individual  and personal
histories
Power   of data given
back to individualises
Considers users
subjectivity
Aimed to empower
through visibility







                                                                                                                                                   11

   Examine Power
   Aspir e  to Empower



        Design Process Questions: How  is power
        distributed across the design team? Whose
        voice matters more and why?   How  can end-
        users’ voices be more fully integrated into
        the design process? Can we build capacity
        in user communities, or enlarge our internal
        perspectives, by employing  a more
        participatory design process?


        Design Output Questions: Can the
        visualization empower   the end-user and/or
        her community,  group, or organization?




D’Ignazio, C., & Klein, L. F. (2016).        Feminist Data                                                                    12
Visualization      .

                     Consider
                     Context



“Each week,  and for a year, we
collected and measured a
particular type of data about our
lives, used this data to make a
drawing   on a postcard-sized
sheet of paper, and then
dropped the postcard in an
English “postbox” (Stefanie) or
an American     “mailbox”
(Giorgia)!”  (Dear Data, n.d.)

                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    13

   Consider Context

         Design Process Questions: How  can we
         leverage human-centered design and
         participatory  design methods to learn about
         and with  our end-users, including learning
         more about their culture, history,
         circumstances, and worldviews?   How  can
         we let these insights shape our design
         practice and change our notions about what
         constitutes “good” information design?


         Design Output Questions: What kinds of
         terminology, symbols, and cultural artifacts
         have meaning to end-users, and how can
         we  incorporate those into our designs?
         What might we  learn if we  were  to visualize
         “messy” data? How do we take context into
         account in the assessment of
         visualizations?
D’Ignazio, C., & Klein, L. F. (2016).        Feminist Data
Visualization      .                                                                                                                 14

                                                                                                                16

   Le  gitimise Body
   and Af  fect

Design Process Questions: How  can we  leverage
embodied and affective experience  to enhance
visualization design and engage users? What kinds
of expertise might we  need on our design team in
order to do that? (e.g. fine art, graphic design,
animation, or communication specialists)


Design Output Questions: What kinds of embodied
and affective experience  has meaning to end-
users? Should we consider tactile, experiential, or
social ways  of accessing the data visualization?
Can we  consider visualization  outputs in an
expanded  field, such as data murals, data
sculptures, public walks, quilts, and installations?




D’Ignazio, C., & Klein, L. F. (2016).        Feminist Data                                                                           17
Visualization      .

   Make Labour
   V isible



         Design Process Questions: Can the team
         work backwards  from the given data to
         document their provenance and talk to their
         caregivers? Has the team discussed roles,
         responsibilities,  and credit in advance of
         publication?


         Design Output Questions: Is it feasible to
         provide a metadata visualization that shows
         the provenance of the data and their
         stakeholders (caregivers) at each step?
         Have we properly attributed work on the
         project?



D’Ignazio, C., & Klein, L. F. (2016).        Feminist Data                                                                           18
Visualization      .

   Make Labour
   V isible



          Related to Data Provenance:
          Technically -                      the ‘chain of custody’
          ownership  and processes of data
          Practically  – crediting sources and data
          wo r k
          Critically – examining  the invisible labour
          within  data science
          Further critical inquiry – emotional labour
          and care work







D’Ignazio, C., & Klein, L. F. (2023).                Data Feminism      . MIT                                                                    19
Press.

                 20

    T  he Body and
    D  at  a

 Extending the idea of embodiment Hill (2020) explores how  bodies are
 incorporated or missing from data visualizations:
 Bodies are extracted – data is extracted about bodies, rendered as ‘objective’ and
 removed from material bodies
 Bodies are absent – reinforces biases by leaving out particular groups of people
 Bodies go unaccounted – some bodies are more likely to be documented than
 others for particular reasons, leaving to incomplete data sources
 Bodies are rendered invisible – data visualisations  are interpretations of the world
 and who  makes them is important to consider – they often represent dominant
 viewpoints
 The body  of the viewer – conventions of data visualizations can misinterpret data
 and create misinformation





Hill, R. L. (2020). W hat is at stake in data visualization?: A feminist critique of the rhetorical power of data visualizations                                                                                                               in the
media. In M.           Engebretsen                                                       & H. Kennedy (Eds.),           Data Visualization in Society                      (pp. 391–                                                                                                            406). Amsterdam   University     Press. 21
https://doi.org/10.2307/j.ctvzgb8c7.29

Summar      y






     Consider various bodies
     Consider situated knowledge – the social,
     cultural, and material conditions that shape
     how  we  understand the world
     Consider diverse subjects, rather than
     objective truth (impossible to objective!)
     Consider who  is represented in production
     and who isn’t
     Consider who  is represented in data and
     who isn’t
